dataset_info.name: CDSCO Hackathon — Synthetic Inspection Notes Dataset
dataset_info.compliance: CDSCO Schedule M, MDR 2017, DPDP Act 2023 (anonymised)
dataset_info.note: All firm names, addresses and licence numbers are fictitious. Created for testing only.

records.firm_address: Plot 42, Industrial Area Phase II, Baddi, Himachal Pradesh 173205
records.inspectors: Dr. R. Sharma, Mr. A. Mehta
records.raw_text: Inspection was carried out at the manufacturing premises of Alpha Formulations Pvt Ltd on 12th March 2024. The facility manufactures oral solid dosage forms including tablets and capsules. During the inspection, the following observations were recorded. The manufacturing area was found to be adequately maintained with proper air handling units in place. However, the qualification records for three HVAC units were found to be outdated and had not been reviewed since January 2022. The water system validation report was available but the last periodic revalidation was overdue by 6 months. The batch manufacturing records for Paracetamol 500mg tablets Batch No. ALF-2024-P-0112 were reviewed. It was noted that in-process checks were not completed at all specified intervals, with two entries missing for the granulation stage. The QC laboratory was found to be generally compliant. However, two analysts were found working without valid training records for the analytical procedures they were performing. The SOP for handling out-of-specification results was last revised in 2021 and does not reflect current CDSCO guidance. The pest control records were available and found to be in order. Stability study data for three products was reviewed and found satisfactory. The finished product storage area temperature mapping exercise was overdue. A review of the complaint register revealed 4 product quality complaints in the past 12 months, of which two had not been investigated within the prescribed timeline. The CAPA register was reviewed; 7 CAPAs were pending closure beyond their target dates.
records.observations: 1. HVAC qualification records outdated. 2. Water system revalidation overdue. 3. Incomplete in-process BMR entries. 4. Analysts working without valid training records. 5. OOS SOP outdated. 6. Finished product storage temperature mapping overdue. 7. Complaints not investigated within timeline. 8. 7 open CAPAs past due.
records.capa: 1. Update and review all HVAC qualification records within 30 days. 2. Complete water system revalidation immediately. 3. Retrain all QC analysts and update training records. 4. Revise OOS SOP to align with current CDSCO guidance within 45 days. 5. Conduct storage area temperature mapping. 6. Investigate all pending complaints and close within 15 days. 7. Close all overdue CAPAs with root cause analysis.

records.firm_address: Survey No. 88, Genome Valley, Hyderabad, Telangana 500078
records.inspectors: Dr. P. Nair, Dr. S. Kulkarni, Ms. T. Reddy
records.raw_text: Pre-approval inspection of Beta Biologics Ltd was conducted to assess compliance with Schedule M-III requirements for sterile biological products. The aseptic fill-finish area was inspected and found to meet ISO Class 5 requirements during media fill simulations. The cell bank documentation was reviewed and found to be compliant with ICH Q5D guidelines. However, the environmental monitoring programme for cleanrooms showed an exceedance in total viable count at one sampling point in the filling corridor on two occasions in the past six months. The root cause analysis for these exceedances was incomplete. The bioburden testing programme for raw materials was reviewed. Three batches of a critical excipient had been released without completed bioburden results due to a laboratory instrument breakdown. The instrument maintenance logs confirmed the breakdown but there was no formal risk assessment conducted for those batches. The validated cleaning procedure for the multi-product bioreactor showed inadequate removal of residual host cell proteins as evidenced by a recent change control review. A formal re-validation was initiated but not yet completed. The cold chain management system for the final drug substance was found to be robust with continuous temperature monitoring and complete chain of custody documentation. Personnel qualification records for all critical aseptic processing operators were reviewed and found to be complete and current.
records.observations: 1. Environmental monitoring exceedances with incomplete root cause analysis. 2. Three batches released without complete bioburden testing. 3. Cleaning validation inadequate for HCP removal. 4. No risk assessment for batches released during instrument breakdown.
records.capa: 1. Complete root cause analysis for EM exceedances and implement corrective measures within 21 days. 2. Conduct retrospective risk assessment for the 3 released batches and quarantine if risk is unacceptable. 3. Complete cleaning re-validation for bioreactor within 60 days. 4. Implement procedure requiring risk assessment before release during instrument downtime.

records.firm_address: Unit 7, SEZ Phase I, Kochi, Kerala 682024
records.inspectors: Mr. V. Pillai, Dr. M. Suresh
records.raw_text: Complaint-based inspection was triggered following three adverse event reports submitted to CDSCO concerning failure of the firm's implantable cardiac monitoring device. The inspection was conducted at the manufacturing facility. The design history file for the device was reviewed. While the DHF was generally well-maintained, the latest design change implemented in February 2024 to modify the battery sealing mechanism had not undergone a formal risk assessment update in accordance with ISO 14971. The post-market surveillance plan was reviewed and found to be inadequate; it lacked specific criteria for trending of complaints and did not define escalation thresholds for adverse event reporting. Review of the batch records for the lots involved in the reported adverse events revealed that one lot had passed the finished device testing with an out-of-tolerance reading for the impedance test, but the deviation had been closed without adequate investigation, referencing only a measurement uncertainty note. The supplier qualification file for the battery supplier showed that the last audit was conducted in 2021 and no annual review had been performed since. The sterilisation validation records were reviewed and found to be complete. The cleanroom maintenance logs were in order.
records.observations: 1. Design change without updated risk assessment per ISO 14971. 2. Inadequate post-market surveillance plan. 3. Out-of-tolerance impedance result closed without proper investigation. 4. Battery supplier audit overdue (last in 2021).
records.capa: 1. Immediately update risk assessment for the February 2024 design change per ISO 14971. 2. Revise post-market surveillance plan to include complaint trending and adverse event thresholds. 3. Re-investigate the out-of-tolerance impedance lot with full CAPA. 4. Conduct supplier audit of battery supplier within 90 days.

records.inspectors: Ms. K. Shah, Mr. D. Joshi
records.raw_text: Surveillance inspection was conducted at Delta Ayurvedic Pharmaceuticals. The firm manufactures classical Ayurvedic formulations and proprietary medicines. The raw material testing area was inspected. Heavy metal testing for raw herbs including lead, cadmium, arsenic and mercury is conducted as per AYUSH standards. Records showed that all tested raw materials were within prescribed limits. However, the pesticide residue testing was found to be outsourced to a laboratory that had not been evaluated for competence against NABL criteria as required. The manufacturing floor was found to be clean and organised. The firm uses copper and bronze vessels for certain classical preparations as prescribed in the Sharangdhara Samhita. Documentation for traditional texts referenced in the manufacturing process was available. Review of the product labelling for three proprietary Ayurvedic products revealed that the label claims were not substantiated with adequate clinical or bibliographic evidence as per the Drugs and Magic Remedies Act. The pharmacovigilance system for Ayurvedic products was rudimentary; adverse drug reaction reporting was not systematic, and no reports had been filed with the NCC-Ayurveda pharmacovigilance programme in the past two years despite the firm having over 50,000 units in distribution.
records.observations: 1. Outsourced pesticide residue testing laboratory not evaluated for NABL competence. 2. Label claims on 3 products unsubstantiated. 3. Inadequate pharmacovigilance system — no ADR reports filed.
records.capa: 1. Qualify or replace the pesticide residue testing laboratory within 60 days. 2. Review and revise label claims for three products, removing unsubstantiated claims. 3. Establish formal ADR collection and reporting system; report all pending cases to NCC-Ayurveda within 30 days.

records.firm_address: Block C, Medical Complex, Pune, Maharashtra 411001
records.inspectors: Dr. A. Kulkarni, Dr. N. Desai
records.raw_text: Follow-up inspection was conducted to verify closure of deficiencies identified during the previous routine inspection of October 2023. The firm collects, processes and distributes blood components including packed red blood cells, platelet concentrates and fresh frozen plasma. Review of the CAPA register against October 2023 deficiencies showed that 6 of 9 CAPAs had been effectively closed with supporting evidence. Three CAPAs remained open: firstly, the apheresis donor screening protocol had been updated but staff training was still in progress with only 4 of 11 apheresis nurses trained; secondly, the temperature mapping of the blood cold room had been conducted but the mapping report had not been reviewed and approved by the Quality Assurance Manager; thirdly, the emergency generator backup validation for the blood bank refrigerators had been rescheduled twice and not yet completed. Newly observed during this inspection: the mandatory serological testing for Hepatitis B surface antigen for one lot of packed RBCs (Lot EPB-2025-RBC-0034) was found to have been performed using a test kit that was 18 days past its expiry date. The lot had not yet been issued. The firm immediately quarantined the lot upon identification of this finding. All donor records, consent forms and deferral records reviewed were found to be complete and compliant with National Blood Policy.
records.observations: 1. Three CAPAs from Oct 2023 still open (apheresis training, cold room mapping approval, generator validation). 2. Expired test kit used for mandatory HBsAg testing on Lot EPB-2025-RBC-0034 (quarantined on identification).
records.capa: 1. Complete apheresis nurse training within 15 days. 2. Obtain QA approval of cold room mapping report within 7 days. 3. Complete emergency generator validation within 30 days. 4. Retest quarantined lot EPB-2025-RBC-0034 with valid test kits. 5. Review test kit expiry checking procedure and implement mandatory dual-check.

records.firm_address: Plot 18, MIDC Industrial Area, Nashik, Maharashtra 422010
records.inspectors: Ms. P. Bhosale, Mr. R. Patil
records.raw_text: Routine inspection was carried out at Zeta Cosmetics Manufacturing Ltd, which manufactures skin care creams, hair oils, and colour cosmetics. The manufacturing premises were inspected for compliance with the Drugs and Cosmetics Act 1940 and Schedule M-II. The raw material storage area was found to be adequately maintained with proper segregation of approved and quarantined materials. However, the certificate of analysis for three imported raw material batches including a UV filter ingredient was not verified against the approved supplier specification before use in production. The filling line for liquid cosmetics was inspected. Microbiological challenge testing records for two preservative systems used in water-based formulations were not available, raising concerns about product safety margins. The stability programme was reviewed. Three products in the sunscreen range lacked ongoing stability data beyond 12 months despite having a claimed shelf life of 36 months. Product labels for four SKUs were reviewed and two were found to carry claims such as dermatologically tested and hypoallergenic without supporting documentation on file. The complaint handling system was reviewed; consumer complaints received through e-commerce platforms were not being systematically captured into the internal complaint register.
records.observations: 1. Raw material CoA not verified against approved specifications for 3 imported batches. 2. Microbiological challenge testing records missing for 2 preservative systems. 3. Stability data incomplete for sunscreen products beyond 12 months. 4. Unsubstantiated label claims on 2 SKUs. 5. E-commerce consumer complaints not captured in complaint register.
records.capa: 1. Verify and document CoA for the 3 imported raw material batches within 14 days; quarantine if non-compliant. 2. Complete microbiological challenge testing for both preservative systems within 60 days. 3. Initiate 36-month stability studies for sunscreen range immediately. 4. Remove or substantiate hypoallergenic and dermatologically tested claims within 30 days. 5. Integrate e-commerce complaint channel into the central complaint management system.

records.firm_address: Ground Floor, Research Block, AIIMS Campus, Bhopal, Madhya Pradesh 462020
records.inspectors: Dr. S. Verma, Dr. L. Patel, Ms. R. Gupta
records.raw_text: GCP inspection was conducted at Eta Clinical Research Centre, which is currently participating as an investigator site in three ongoing Phase III clinical trials regulated under the New Drugs and Clinical Trials Rules 2019. The inspection covered trial TS-2023-ONCO-04 for a novel oncology investigational product. The investigator site file was reviewed. The protocol deviations log showed 14 deviations recorded over the past 12 months, of which 3 were classified as major deviations. Two of the three major deviations had not been reported to the sponsor or the IEC within the required 7-day window. The informed consent process was examined. A review of 8 participant consent forms revealed that 2 participants had signed forms that were not the current approved version; the superseded version lacked updated risk information from a protocol amendment issued in November 2023. The investigational product accountability log was reviewed and found complete. However, temperature logs for the IP storage refrigerator showed two excursions beyond the permitted range of 2 to 8 degrees Celsius, each lasting more than 4 hours, with no documented assessment of product impact. The adverse event reporting records were examined. One serious adverse event that occurred in January 2024 had been reported to CDSCO but the causality assessment by the principal investigator was missing from the regulatory submission file.
records.observations: 1. Two major protocol deviations not reported to sponsor or IEC within 7 days. 2. Two participants consented using superseded ICF version missing updated risk information. 3. Two IP storage temperature excursions with no documented product impact assessment. 4. SAE causality assessment missing from CDSCO submission file.
records.capa: 1. Report the two unreported major deviations to sponsor and IEC within 48 hours. 2. Re-consent the two affected participants using the current approved ICF version. 3. Conduct and document product impact assessment for the two temperature excursions; escalate to sponsor. 4. Submit the missing causality assessment for the January 2024 SAE to CDSCO within 7 days.

records.firm_address: Warehouse 9, Free Trade Zone, Jawaharlal Nehru Port, Navi Mumbai 400707
records.inspectors: Mr. B. Singh, Ms. A. Iyer
records.raw_text: Surveillance inspection was conducted at the import warehouse of Theta Import and Distribution Co, which holds an import licence for finished formulations sourced from manufacturers in the United States and Germany. The inspection focused on post-import quality assurance activities. The approved supplier list was reviewed and found current with audit records available for all 4 overseas manufacturers. However, the import testing protocol required under Rule 75 of the Drugs and Cosmetics Rules was not being consistently applied; records showed that 6 out of 22 import consignments received in the past financial year had been released without full Rule 75 testing, with the firm citing reference to the overseas manufacturer's Certificate of Analysis. The cold chain records for temperature-sensitive biological medicines were reviewed. GPS temperature logger data for 3 consignments showed a break in the cold chain during last-mile delivery from the customs bonded warehouse, with temperatures reaching 12 degrees Celsius for a shipment requiring 2 to 8 degrees Celsius storage. No quarantine action had been taken for these consignments. The approved labelling for 2 imported products was compared against the actual labels on received stock and discrepancies were found in the dosage instructions section.
records.observations: 1. 6 of 22 import consignments released without mandatory Rule 75 testing. 2. Cold chain breach detected for 3 biological medicine consignments — no quarantine action taken. 3. Label discrepancies found in dosage instructions for 2 imported products.
records.capa: 1. Immediately quarantine the 3 consignments with documented cold chain breach pending quality assessment. 2. Retrospectively review and document the 6 consignments released without Rule 75 testing; recall if required. 3. Implement a mandatory Rule 75 testing gate before release of all import consignments. 4. Correct label discrepancies and recall affected stock of the 2 mis-labelled products.

records.firm_address: Plot 55, Surgical Hub, Aligarh, Uttar Pradesh 202001
records.inspectors: Dr. C. Sharma, Mr. T. Mishra
records.raw_text: Pre-approval inspection was conducted at Iota Surgical Instruments Pvt Ltd prior to grant of manufacturing licence for Class A sterile surgical instruments including scalpels, forceps and retractors. The quality management system was assessed against MDR 2017 and ISO 13485:2016 requirements. The design and development documentation was reviewed. Design validation records for the sterile barrier system used in final packaging were found to be complete and compliant with ISO 11607. The manufacturing process for stainless steel instruments was inspected. The electrolytic passivation process, which is critical for corrosion resistance and biocompatibility, was found to be lacking a validated process with defined critical parameters and acceptance criteria. Operators were performing the passivation process based on informal instructions rather than a controlled SOP. The sterilisation process for finished instruments was found to be outsourced to a contract steriliser. The contract review and technical agreement with the steriliser were available. However, the sterilisation validation report provided by the contractor had not been reviewed and accepted by the firm's own quality team. The labelling system was reviewed and found to be compliant with MDR 2017 labelling requirements. Traceability records from raw material receipt through to finished goods dispatch were reviewed and found to be adequate.
records.observations: 1. Electrolytic passivation process not validated — no defined critical parameters or acceptance criteria. 2. Operators performing passivation without a controlled SOP. 3. Contract steriliser validation report not reviewed or accepted by firm's quality team.
records.capa: 1. Develop and execute a full validation protocol for the electrolytic passivation process including critical parameters and acceptance criteria within 90 days. 2. Create and implement a controlled SOP for the passivation process; train all operators. 3. Formally review and accept the contract steriliser validation report; raise any gaps with the contractor within 30 days.

records.firm_address: Survey 77, Herbal Park, Mandsaur, Madhya Pradesh 458001
records.inspectors: Mr. A. Tiwari, Ms. S. Rao
records.raw_text: Complaint-based inspection was initiated following consumer reports of foreign matter contamination found in Ashwagandha root powder supplied by Kappa Herbal Extracts Pvt Ltd. The firm processes and packages standardised herbal extracts for Ayurvedic formulation manufacturers. The raw material receiving area was inspected. The incoming quality control procedure for raw herbs requires visual inspection and identity testing as per API standards. However, review of receiving records showed that identity testing using TLC or HPTLC was performed for only 11 of 30 raw material lots received in the past six months; the remaining 19 lots were released based solely on supplier CoA. The processing area was found to be generally clean. The sieving and milling equipment was inspected and metal contamination controls including magnetic separators were in place and functional. However, the cleaning validation for shared equipment used to process multiple herb species had not been performed; there was no data to demonstrate absence of cross-contamination between successive products. The finished product testing included assay for key marker compounds and microbiological testing. Aflatoxin testing, which is mandatory for Ashwagandha under AYUSH guidelines, was being performed but the method validation records for the in-house HPLC method used were incomplete, with recovery studies not documented.
records.observations: 1. Identity testing not performed for 19 of 30 incoming raw material lots. 2. Cleaning validation absent for shared processing equipment. 3. Aflatoxin HPLC method validation incomplete — recovery studies missing.
records.capa: 1. Immediately perform TLC identity testing on all 19 uninspected raw material lots; quarantine those failing identity. 2. Develop and execute cleaning validation for shared equipment within 60 days. 3. Complete HPLC method validation for aflatoxin testing including full recovery studies within 45 days.

records.inspectors: Dr. M. Kapoor, Dr. V. Arora, Ms. J. Singh
records.raw_text: Routine inspection was conducted at Lambda Plasma Fractionation Centre, which manufactures albumin, immunoglobulins and clotting factor concentrates from pooled human plasma. The facility is regulated under Schedule C of the Drugs and Cosmetics Act. The plasma sourcing records were reviewed. All plasma pools were sourced from licensed blood banks and quarantine plasma management was in place with adequate hold periods. Viral inactivation steps including solvent-detergent treatment and nanofiltration were validated and records for each production batch confirmed the steps were completed within validated parameters. However, during review of production batch records for Intravenous Immunoglobulin, it was found that the depth filtration step for two batches had been completed at a flow rate 15 percent above the validated upper limit due to a filter clogging event. The deviations had been documented but the retrospective impact assessment on product quality had not been completed. The environmental monitoring data for the Grade A filling isolator showed that particulate counts had exceeded alert limits on three occasions in the past quarter. Corrective actions had been documented but trend analysis had not been performed on the cumulative data. The finished product release testing records were reviewed and found complete. The cold chain distribution system was verified to be compliant with WHO guidelines for temperature-sensitive biologicals.
records.observations: 1. Two IVIG batches processed at filtration flow rate exceeding validated upper limit — impact assessment incomplete. 2. Grade A isolator particulate count exceeded alert limits 3 times — no trend analysis performed. 3. Deviations documented but quality impact assessments not closed.
records.capa: 1. Complete retrospective quality impact assessment for the 2 IVIG batches with out-of-range filtration flow rates within 21 days. 2. Perform trend analysis on all Grade A environmental monitoring data for the past 12 months. 3. Implement automated alerting and mandatory trend review for all environmental monitoring exceedances.

records.inspectors: Dr. A. Rawat, Ms. P. Negi
records.raw_text: Follow-up inspection was conducted at Mu Derma Cosmetics Pvt Ltd to verify closure of observations raised during the previous inspection of July 2024. The firm manufactures medicated shampoos, anti-acne creams and lip care products. Review of the CAPA register against July 2024 observations showed that 5 of 7 CAPAs had been closed with satisfactory evidence. Two CAPAs remained open: the validation of the in-process pH monitoring system was still pending, and the revised SOP for handling customer returns had not yet been approved by the Quality Head. A new observation was raised during this inspection: the preservative efficacy testing data for a newly launched preservative-free micellar water product was not on file. The firm had launched the product three months prior but efficacy testing had not been initiated. The raw material warehouse was inspected and found to have improved segregation controls since the previous visit. The batch records for 4 products were reviewed and in-process documentation was found to be complete and accurate. The stability chambers were inspected; calibration records were current and temperature uniformity mapping had been completed as required.
records.observations: 1. Two CAPAs from July 2024 still open (pH monitoring validation and customer returns SOP approval). 2. Preservative efficacy testing absent for newly launched preservative-free micellar water product.
records.capa: 1. Complete validation of in-process pH monitoring system within 30 days. 2. Obtain Quality Head approval for the revised customer returns SOP within 14 days. 3. Initiate and complete preservative efficacy testing for the micellar water product within 45 days; consider voluntary hold on distribution pending results.

records.firm_address: Plot 9, Pharma City, Vishakhapatnam, Andhra Pradesh 530032
records.inspectors: Dr. B. Rao, Ms. C. Lakshmi, Mr. P. Reddy
records.raw_text: Pre-approval inspection was conducted at Nu Ophthalmics Ltd prior to consideration of manufacturing licence for sterile ophthalmic eye drops and ointments. The facility was assessed for compliance with Schedule M Part I and the WHO guidelines for ophthalmic preparations. The aseptic processing area for eye drops was inspected. The Grade A laminar flow zones were verified to meet ISO Class 5 requirements based on recent particle count data. The filling line qualification records were reviewed and found to be complete with Installation Qualification, Operational Qualification and Performance Qualification documents available. The water system for Water for Injection production was inspected. The TOC and endotoxin monitoring records for the WFI system showed that results had been within specification for all tests in the past six months. The autoclave validation for terminal sterilisation of certain products was reviewed. Biological indicator logs confirmed adequate sterility assurance levels. However, the container closure integrity testing programme for the multi-dose eye drop bottles was found to have a significant gap: CCIT had been performed using dye ingress method for only 3 of 12 product formulations; the remaining 9 formulations had no CCIT data on file. The personnel gowning qualification records for 4 of 18 cleanroom operators were found to have lapsed.
records.observations: 1. Container closure integrity testing absent for 9 of 12 ophthalmic product formulations. 2. Gowning qualification records lapsed for 4 cleanroom operators.
records.capa: 1. Complete CCIT for all 12 ophthalmic product formulations using a validated method within 90 days; place products without CCIT on hold for commercial distribution. 2. Re-qualify the 4 operators with lapsed gowning qualifications before they re-enter the classified area.

records.firm_address: Tower B, Life Sciences Park, Bengaluru, Karnataka 560100
records.inspectors: Dr. R. Kumar, Ms. S. Nair
records.raw_text: Surveillance inspection was conducted at Xi Diagnostics Pvt Ltd, which manufactures in-vitro diagnostic devices including blood glucose monitoring systems and rapid antigen test kits. The quality management system was assessed for compliance with MDR 2017 and ISO 13485:2016. The design history file for the blood glucose monitoring system was reviewed and found to be comprehensive. The analytical performance validation data including accuracy, precision and linearity were available and met ISO 15197 requirements. The post-market surveillance system was reviewed. A total of 47 field complaints had been received in the past year. Analysis of the complaint data showed a clustering of reports related to erroneous high readings from a specific reagent strip lot. The complaint trend had not been identified through the formal trending process and no field safety corrective action had been initiated. The risk management file was reviewed; the failure mode for reagent strip degradation due to humidity exposure had not been adequately addressed in the risk control measures. The rapid antigen test kit manufacturing area was inspected. The antigen coating process, which is a critical manufacturing step, was found to be monitored using in-process controls. However, the process capability study for the coating uniformity parameter had not been completed. The shelf life studies for both product lines were available and supporting the claimed expiry dates.
records.observations: 1. Complaint trending failure — cluster of erroneous high readings from specific lot not identified or acted upon. 2. Risk management file inadequate for humidity-related reagent strip degradation failure mode. 3. Process capability study for antigen coating uniformity not completed.
records.capa: 1. Immediately investigate the clustering of erroneous high reading complaints; initiate field safety corrective action if risk assessment warrants it within 14 days. 2. Update risk management file to address humidity exposure failure mode with adequate risk controls. 3. Complete process capability study for antigen coating uniformity within 60 days.

records.inspectors: Dr. P. Joshi, Dr. S. Iyer, Mr. A. Nair
records.raw_text: Routine GMP inspection was conducted at Omicron Vaccine Technologies Ltd, which manufactures inactivated influenza vaccines and is in the process of technology transfer for a recombinant subunit vaccine. The inspection covered all aspects of vaccine manufacturing in compliance with Schedule M-III and WHO good manufacturing practices for biological products. The upstream bioreactor processes for antigen production were inspected. Cell culture process parameters including pH, dissolved oxygen and temperature were monitored continuously and records for the past six production runs were reviewed and found to be within validated ranges. The downstream purification process including chromatography and ultrafiltration steps was found to be well documented. The inactivation validation for the influenza antigen using formaldehyde was reviewed and found to comply with WHO requirements. The sterile filtration and aseptic filling operation was inspected. Media fill records for the past 12 months showed two media fill failures, both attributed to operator error. A root cause analysis had been performed and retraining conducted. However, the effectiveness of the corrective action had not been verified through a subsequent qualifying media fill. The lot release testing programme was reviewed. All release tests including potency, sterility, general safety and identity were documented. However, the in-house reference standard used for potency testing had not been re-characterised in accordance with the scheduled 24-month cycle; the current reference standard was 31 months old.
records.observations: 1. Two media fill failures — corrective action effectiveness not verified by qualifying media fill. 2. In-house potency reference standard overdue for re-characterisation by 7 months.
records.capa: 1. Conduct a qualifying media fill to demonstrate effectiveness of the corrective action for media fill failures before resuming commercial filling. 2. Immediately initiate re-characterisation of the in-house potency reference standard; do not use for release testing until re-characterised or a bridging study is completed.
